fields.Title: Depreciated Laptop and Desktop Inventory Report
fields.Why:
Problem: No existing list of depreciated assets (4 years and older).

Outcome: A validated list of devices aged 4+ years is established, enabling better decisions for replacement and lifecycle planning.

Success Criteria:

  • A complete list of devices aged 4 years and above is generated based on depreciation value (USD 200 and below) in the Asset Manager.
  • For assets without a recorded depreciation value, the purchase date is used as the basis for determining age.
  • The list includes essential details such as assigned user, device name, and/or asset tag.
  • The list serves as a reference for identifying devices for potential replacement or procurement.
  • The output is clear, organized, and easily understandable for IT and relevant stakeholders.
fields.AcceptanceCriteria: 1. All deliverables delivered and accepted
fields.Schedule:
Action Taken:

  1. Exported asset data from Asset Manager into Excel
  2. Filtered assets to include only laptops and desktops
  3. Filtered devices with depreciation value of USD 200 and below.

fields.Title: Networked Devices Netbox Encoding
fields.Why:
Problem / Need / Request:
SSD office networked devices are not yet fully documented in NetBox. This limits the team’s ability to track changes to networked devices, understand how devices are connected, and identify which links each device is connected to.

Intended Outcome:
NetBox becomes the working record for SSD office networked devices, including device model information, a reference to Asset Manager, and documentation of device connectivity and linked connections where applicable.

Success / What Acceptance Will Check:
An exported list of devices from NetBox matches all networked devices existing in the network.

Scope Boundaries

In Scope:

  • Encode all SSD office networked devices into NetBox
  • Record the proper model for each device
  • Add a way to reference Asset Manager for each device
  • Document how networked devices are connected
  • Document which links devices are connected to

Out of Scope:

  • Non-networked devices
  • Changes to production network design not required for documentation
  • Asset Manager replacement or redesign

Assumptions:

  • NetBox is available for use
  • Existing networked devices can be identified and verified
  • Asset Manager references are available for linking or lookup

Constraints:

  • Accuracy depends on the completeness of the current network inventory
  • Connectivity documentation depends on being able to verify actual device and link relationships
fields.Deliverables:
DeliverableDescriptionDelivery OwnerAcceptance AuthorityAcceptance Evidence / FocusStatus
1. Networked devices encoded into NetBoxNetworked devices are encoded into NetBox with proper model information and a way to reference Asset Manager, with device and link connectivity documented where applicableEsmeraldo ArdienteRyann MicuaExported list of devices from NetBox matches all networked devices existing in the networkDraft

Risks, Dependencies, and Support

Dependencies / Prerequisites:

  • Access to NetBox
  • Access to current network device information
  • Access to Asset Manager reference data

Access Requirements:

  • Permission to view and update NetBox records
  • Access to network inventory and topology details
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
Closure and Acceptance

Closure Criteria:

  • All identified SSD office networked devices are recorded in NetBox
  • Each recorded device has the proper model
  • Each recorded device has a usable Asset Manager reference
  • Device and link connectivity are documented where applicable
  • NetBox export reconciles with the actual networked devices in the network

Acceptance Confirmation:
To be completed by the Acceptance Authority after validation.

fields.Title: Campus Network Rack Clean-Up
fields.RelWorkBrief:
ITS Work Brief - Campus Network Clean-Up.docx

Related documents:

  • Appendix A: Annex A - Bill of Materials.docx
  • Appendix B: Annex B - Annual Cleanup Routine Schedule (5-year plan).docx
  • Appendix C: Annex C - Network Cabinet PMS Cleaning Checklist.docx
  • Photos: Photo Documentations
  • Digital Copy:  Cabinet Rack PMS Checklist.pdf
  • Consolidated: Rack & Equipment PMS Cleaning Checklist.xlsx
  • Closure Report: Closure Report WB-10.pdf
fields.Why: All network cabinets across the campus, including those located at the back of houses, are cleaned, organized, and maintained according to standard procedures.
fields.Requirements:
Requirements:

  1. All network cabinets across the campus, including residential areas, must be identified and included.
  2. Each cabinet must undergo cleaning and preventive maintenance using the standard PMS Cleaning Checklist.
  3. Completion of the Rack & Equipment PMS Cleaning Checklist for every cabinet.
  4. Creation of an Excel file documenting all cleaned network cabinets.
  5. Recording of key details such as location, date cleaned, and assigned personnel
  6. Use of appropriate tools, materials, and PPE during cleaning activities
  7. Validation and approval of completed checklists and inventory by the Network Administrator
In Scope
  1. Cleaning and dust removal of all network cabinets across the campus
  2. Basic organization of equipment and cable management within cabinets
  3. Completion and documentation of PMS Cleaning Checklists
  4. Creation of a campus-wide inventory of cleaned network cabinets (Excel file)
  5. Coverage includes cabinets located in offices, facilities, and residential/back-of-house areas
Out of Scope
  1. Repair, replacement, or upgrade of network equipment
  2. Structural modifications of cabinets or infrastructure
  3. Advanced troubleshooting or reconfiguration of network devices
  4. Procurement beyond basic cleaning materials and tools
Constraints
  1. Need to coordinate with users to avoid service disruption during cleaning
  2. Environmental factors (dust levels, weather conditions, or pests in certain areas)

fields.Title: Review of IT Helpdesk Playbooks
fields.RelWorkBrief:
Related Links: 
Current Helpdesk Playbooks: HelpDesk
Playbook Template: playbook-template.md 

Ceate a Playbook: create-a-playbook.md
fields.Why:
IT Helpdesk playbooks guide the handling of incidents, requests, onboarding, and offboarding processes. Over time, system changes and evolving requirements can make these playbooks outdated or inconsistent.

A review is necessary to ensure procedures remain accurate, standardized, and aligned with current tools and workflows, improving service efficiency and consistency across the IT team.

Outcome:

  • Assessment report on IT Helpdesk playbooks, covering usability and unused playbooks.
fields.AcceptanceCriteria: 1. All deliverables are completed in accordance with the defined requirements.
fields.Requirements:
Requirements:
  1. Review all existing IT Helpdesk playbooks (including onboarding and offboarding)
  2. Identify outdated, incomplete, or inconsistent procedure.

Scope

  1. Review of all existing IT Helpdesk playbooks
  2. Coverage of incident handling, service requests, onboarding, and offboarding processes.
  3. Review of clarity, accuracy, and usability of playbooks

Out of Scope:

  1. Updating of playbooks
  2. Major process redesign.

Constraints:

  1. Urgent tickets and walk-ins' request.
fields.Who:
OJT will try the playbooks and note any comments suggestions or constraints that they will encounter

Mervin Mani will do the validating and final review of playbooks that the OJT reviewed and tested.
fields.Schedule:
April 14-20 : OJTs review and testing phase

April 21-24: Mervin M. review and validation phase

April 27: Final submission for Acceptance Review (IT Team Review)

fields.Title: Approved MVP Backlog - Retirement System
fields.RelWorkBrief:
Phase 1: Foundation

ArtifactWhy
Functional CapabilitiesDefines the business-level scope. All downstream artifacts should align to it.

 

Phase 2: Structuring

ArtifactWhy
Solution ModulesOrganizes the solution into logical components.
User Roles and Access ModelDefines actors and permissions that shape solution behavior.
Data Asset RegistryDefines the core data needed by the solution.

 

Phase 3: Definition

ArtifactWhy
Use CasesDefines the key interaction flows.
Business RulesDefines the logic and constraints behind those flows.

 

Phase 4: Specification

ArtifactWhy
Selected Use CasesNarrows scope to what will be implemented.
Feature SpecificationConverts all upstream decisions into build-ready detail.

fields.Title: LHC Landing Page
fields.Why:
Background: Life Hope Center will deploy an online Guest Booking Platform.
Today, LHC has no landing page or web presence where users can access that platform.

Outcome: Create an LHC landing page that establishes an initial web presence and includes a clear section or call-to-action that links users to the Guest Booking Platform.
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
Core Criteria

  • Landing page is live and accessible via public URL
  • Page displays LHC name, logo, and approved content correctly
  • Guest Booking section is clearly visible without scrolling confusion
  • Call-to-action (button/link) to booking platform is prominent and labeled clearly
  • Clicking the link redirects to the correct Guest Booking Platform URL
  • Page works on both desktop and mobile devices
  • Page loads successfully under normal conditions

 

Quality Criteria

  • Content is approved by LHC stakeholders
  • Design is aligned with LHC branding
  • No broken links or missing elements
  • No placeholder or draft content remain

 

Technical Criteria

  • Page is deployed with HTTPS (secure access)
  • Domain and hosting are properly configured
  • No critical defects impacting usability
  • Basic cross-browser compatibility (e.g., Chrome, Safari, Edge)

 

Handoever Criteria

  • Booking link tested and confirmed working
  • Ownership for page updates is clearly assigned
  • Access to update content is provided (if applicable)
fields.Requirements:
Requirements

Business Requirements

  • Establish an online presence for Life Hope Center
  • Enable guests to access the booking platform
  • Improve accessibility and credibility of LHC

Stakeholder Requirements

  • Guests need a simple way to find and access booking
  • LHC needs a page that reflects its identity
  • Admin needs minimal effort to maintain content

Solution Requirements

  • Provide a landing page accessible via public URL
  • Include a clear call-to-action linking to booking platform
  • Display basic LHC information
  • Support mobile and desktop access

Transition Requirements

  • Domain and hosting must be set up
  • Content and branding must be prepared and approved
  • Booking platform URL must be finalized
  • Page must be deployed and made publicly accessible


Scope

IN

  • Design and build a single LHC landing page
  • Display LHC name, logo, and basic branding
  • Include introductory information about Life Hope Center
  • Provide a clear call-to-action linking to the Guest Booking Platform
  • Ensure booking link is visible and easy to access
  • Enable access via a public web URL
  • Support mobile and desktop responsiveness
  • Set up basic hosting and domain (if required)
  • Implement secure access (HTTPS)

OUT

  • Development or modification of the Guest Booking Platform
  • Multi-page website or full website build
  • User accounts, login, or registration features
  • Advanced integrations (CRM, payments, analytics, etc.)
  • SEO strategy or digital marketing activities
  • Blog, events, or additional content features
  • Ongoing maintenance and support beyond initial launch
  • Complex UI/UX features or custom applications

 

Constraints

  • No existing web presence
    Starting from zero (no domain, hosting, or baseline design)
  • Limited scope (single landing page only)
    Must avoid expansion into full website or additional features
  • Content availability and approval
    LHC must provide and approve logo, text, and branding
  • Resource constraint
    Limited design/development capacity may restrict complexity
  • Technology constraint
    Must use simple, standard web technologies (no complex architecture)
  • Security baseline requirement
    Must implement HTTPS (cannot launch without it)
  • Stakeholder alignment
    Decisions may be delayed if ownership or approvals are unclear

fields.Title: Retirement System - Civil Status Research
fields.Why:
Background
The retirement process requires accurate tracking of applicant civil status.
Civil status may change over time due to life events.
These changes impact eligibility, benefits, and records.

Current understanding is fragmented.
No standard mapping of transitions and impacts exists.

Outcome
A complete and structured analysis of civil status changes.
Clear mapping of transitions, triggers, and impacts.
Input to process improvement, policy clarification, and system design.
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
• All major civil statuses identified
  • All logical transitions mapped
  • Each transition has:
    • Trigger event
    • Required documents
    • Defined impact
  • No conflicting or duplicate transitions
  • Output is structured in tables
fields.Requirements:
Scope

  • Retirement applicants only
  • Civil status changes before and during application
  • High-level process view only

Out of Scope

  • System development
  • Legal interpretation beyond documented rules

Constraints

  • Limited access to real case data
  • Dependency on Retirement SME availability
  • Time-bound OJT engagement

fields.Title: RAID Template Creation
fields.Why:
Background

Current RAID management is maintained in a single consolidated log.
This structure mixes assumptions, risks, issues, and dependencies into one dataset.

This creates the following constraints:

  • Reduced clarity between forward-looking vs active items
  • Limited ability to apply tailored management actions per type
  • Weak signal for decision-making and escalation
  • Increased effort to filter, report, and govern effectively

Based on the defined mental model, each RAID component represents a distinct concept with different lifecycles, ownership patterns, and response strategies.

A single-log structure does not reflect these differences.
This leads to confusion in usage and reduces governance effectiveness.

To improve control, transparency, and actionability, the RAID structure needs to be realigned to its core components.

 

Intended Outcome

Establish four distinct, purpose-built logs:

Log TypePurposeOutcome
Assumptions LogCapture and track validated/unvalidated assumptionsClear visibility of conditions that may impact delivery
Risks LogIdentify and manage uncertain future eventsProactive mitigation and reduced exposure
Issues LogTrack active problems requiring resolutionFaster resolution and accountability
Dependencies LogManage inter-team or external relianceImproved coordination and reduced delays

 

Target State

  • Each log has:
    • Clear definition and entry criteria
    • Standard fields aligned to its purpose
    • Defined ownership and review cadence
  • Governance is improved through:
    • Clear escalation paths per log type
    • Focused reporting (no mixed signals)
    • Better prioritization of actions
  • Delivery teams benefit from:
    • Reduced ambiguity
    • Faster decision-making
    • Improved cross-team coordination

 

Success Criteria

  • 100% of RAID entries correctly classified
  • Reduction in miscategorized items
  • Improved cycle time for issue resolution
  • Increased visibility of high-impact risks and dependencies
  • Positive stakeholder feedback on clarity and usability
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
• All deliverables delivered
  • Playbook is easy to understand and use
  • Spreadsheet template is easy to navigate
fields.Requirements:
Requirement

  • Establish a clear, standardized RAID management approach that improves decision-making, transparency, and control across delivery by separating and managing assumptions, risks, issues, and dependencies distinctly.

 

Scope

In Scope

  • Design of four separate RAID logs
  • Definition of:
    • Data fields per log
    • Entry criteria and classification rules
    • Status lifecycle per log
  • Migration approach:
    • Reclassification of existing RAID entries
    • Data cleanup and normalization
  • Governance setup:
    • Ownership model
    • Review cadence
    • Reporting structure
  • Enablement:
    • Usage guidelines
    • Examples of correct classification

Out of Scope

  • Implementation of new tools or platforms
  • Automation beyond current tool capability
  • Historical data correction beyond reclassification
  • Integration with external systems

 

Constraints

Operational Constraints

  • Must use existing tooling (e.g., current spreadsheet or system)
  • Limited disruption to ongoing project delivery
  • Migration must not impact active issue/risk management

Data Constraints

  • Existing RAID entries may be:
    • Misclassified
    • Duplicated
    • Incomplete
  • No guarantee of consistent historical data quality

Adoption Constraints

  • Varying levels of RAID maturity across teams
  • Resistance to change from single-log approach
  • Training time is limited

Governance Constraints

  • Existing reporting cycles must be maintained

fields.Title: Delivery Owner and Workboard Manager Term of Reference
fields.Why:
Background

The ITS Dept. needs a Terms of Reference document for two internal roles: Delivery Owner and Workboard Manager.

These role names are tailored to the current workflow and are not meant to copy standard textbook titles exactly. At this stage, the working view is:

  • Delivery Owner is close to a Project Manager role.
  • Workboard Manager is close to a Program Manager role.

The purpose of this work is to define these roles clearly in the context of the actual workflow, decision flow, and delivery model used by the team.

The document should make the roles easy to understand, easy to apply, and easy to distinguish from one another.

 

Outcome

Produce a clear Terms of Reference for:

  • Delivery Owner
  • Workboard Manager
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
The work brief will be accepted when:

  • both roles are clearly named and described
  • the purpose of each role is easy to understand
  • the document explains that the roles are tailored to the workflow
  • the brief clearly states what is in scope and out of scope
  • the document is short, practical, and ready for stakeholder review
  • the brief reduces likely confusion about overlap, authority, and coordination
  • the wording is simple enough for non-specialists to understand
fields.Requirements:
Requirements

The work must:

  • define both roles in simple English
  • reflect the real workflow, not only standard theory
  • define role purpose, key responsibilities, boundaries, interfaces, and expected outcomes
  • clarify decision rights, ownership areas, and coordination points

 

Scope

In scope:

  • define the intent of the Delivery Owner role
  • define the intent of the Workboard Manager role
  • identify similarities and differences
  • clarify ownership, coordination, and governance touchpoints
  • describe how the roles interact with teams, stakeholders, and delivery structures

Out of scope:

  • full organization redesign
  • approval of authority changes
  • detailed RACI for all related teams
  • detailed workflow redesign
  • project-specific assignment of named people
  • detailed delivery procedures or playbooks
  • performance management measures for job evaluation

 

Constraints

The work is constrained by the following:

  • role authority may need careful wording if formal governance is not yet finalized
  • the document must stay simple and usable
  • the document must not become too theoretical
  • the brief must support later approval and formalization
  • the role definitions should avoid creating unintended overlap or confusion

fields.Title: Retirement Records Folder Analysis
fields.Why:
Background:

Retirement records are kept in physical folders. The folders seem to be structured, tagged, and color coded. The folders contain change records, updates, service records, certificates, financial transactions that all pertain to the retiree's account. This set of documents need to be understood, analyzed which ones are common, which ones are conditional, what values are expected, etc.

Outcome:

An understanding of what a full retirement records document set (folder) contains along with expected values and required content.
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
1. Digitized retirement records

  1. A digital folder for each retirement record (account) exists. Use the following naming format: [folder #]-[name of retiree]
  2. Folder face is also scanned make sure to show the color coded numbers.
  3. Each document is digitized as a PDF file per document. If a document has multiple pages, it is scanned as a single multi-page file
  4. Save everything by folder inside EXT Retirement System Dev Team -> Design and Planning -> Reference Materials -> Digitized Retirement Records

2. Analysis Report

Analysis report of documents found in sample records.

  • common documents
  • information contained
  • observations, interpretations and insights
  • relationship to other documents, 
  • gaps and open questions for further investigation

fields.Title: Research on Retirement Records Folder Gaps
fields.Why:
Background

The current report gives a working view of the retirement records folder.
It also shows open questions that still need research before later requirements work can be trusted.
These gaps are listed in Section 7 of the report 

Outcome

All Section 7 gaps are answered, or clearly marked unresolved with reason and next step
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
• Every Section 7 gap is addressed
  • Each answer has evidence
  • Confirmed and unresolved items are clearly labeled
  • Output is clear enough to support later requirements work
  • No unsupported assumptions are presented as facts
fields.Requirements:
Requirements

  • Research each gap in Section 7
  • Validate findings with evidence
  • Separate confirmed facts from assumptions
  • Use clear terms and definitions
  • Record any remaining unresolved items

Scope

This work covers research on the Section 7 gaps only:

  • Folder structure and physical identifiers
  • Approval governance and committee structure
  • Service-record interpretation and reconciliation
  • Benefit and status change rules
  • Financial execution and accounting logic
  • Medical-document handling

Constraints

  • Current report is the starting point, not final truth
  • Some answers may need SME input
  • Some practices may not be formally documented
  • Repeated data across documents may cause ambiguity
  • Observed patterns must not be treated as policy without validation

fields.Title: Retirement - Service Record - Data Dictionary
fields.Why:
Background

The Retirement Management System needs clear and reliable service record data.

Service record information may currently use different field names, formats, and meanings across files, systems, and offices.

This work will produce a data dictionary for service record data only.

The data dictionary will help the project team understand what service record fields exist, what each field means, how each field should be formatted, and what issues may affect its use.

This work does not include producing an asset registry yet.

Outcome

A usable service record data dictionary in spreadsheet format that supports the Retirement Management System.
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
• All deliverables are produced
  • Intended outcome achived
fields.Requirements:
• Create a simple spreadsheet-based data dictionary for service record data.
  • Identify the main service record fields needed for the Retirement Management System.
  • Define each field in clear business language.
  • Capture the expected data type or format for each field.
  • Identify whether each field is required or optional, when known.
  • Provide sample values where helpful.
  • Note known data quality issues, such as missing values, duplicates, outdated records, inconsistent formats, or unclear definitions.
  • Identify sensitivity or access concerns for fields that may contain personal or confidential information.
  • Make the spreadsheet easy to review, update, and maintain.
fields.Who:
Work PhaseResponsibleAccountableConsultedInformed
InitiationIT DirectorIT Director Delivery Owner
PlanningDelivery OwnerDelivery OwnerIT Director, Resident Developer, Retirement SME 
ExecutionResident DeveloperDelivery OwnerIT Director 
Monitoring and ControlDelivery OwnerDelivery OwnerIT Director, Resident Developer, Retirement SME 
ClosureDelivery OwnerIT Director Resident Developer, Retirement SME
fields.Scope:
In Scope
- Service record data fields relevant to the Retirement Management System.
- Business definitions for service record fields.
- Expected formats, data types, and sample values.
- Required or optional status of fields, when known.
- Field-level sensitivity notes.
- Known data quality concerns related to service record fields.
- Spreadsheet format for the data dictionary.

Out of Scope
- Asset registry creation.
- Full inventory of data assets, files, systems, or offices.
- Data migration.
- Data cleanup or correction.
- System development.
- Automated system integration.
- Final data governance policy.
- Historical reconstruction of missing records.

Constraints
- Service record data may use different formats and meanings across sources.
- Some field definitions may not be clearly documented.
- Some records may be incomplete, outdated, or inconsistent.
- Some fields may contain sensitive personal information.

fields.Title: LHC Transportation Revisit
fields.Why:
Background

The transportation project was previously placed on hold due to client on leave, shifting priorities, and resource constraints.

The initiative is now being reactivated to validate current business needs and demonstrate the proposed solution through a functional prototype.

The prototype will support stakeholder discussions, workflow validation, and client feedback collection.

Outcome

Deliver a functional prototype that demonstrates key transportation workflows and operational capabilities.

The prototype is intended to:

  • Validate business and user requirements
  • Demonstrate proposed process improvements
  • Identify gaps and refinement opportunities
  • Support alignment on future implementation direction
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
DOD

  1. All deliverables are accepted
  2. Stakeholders are now up to speed with where we are on the project
  3. Feedbacks and recommendations are available for the next iterations
fields.Requirements:
1. Prototype must demonstrate key transportation workflow
  2. Prototype must enable stakeholder review and discussion
  3. Prototype must help identify gaps, missing requirements, process issues, and refinement opportunities

fields.Title: House 143 Access Point Installation
fields.Why:
Background:
There is currently no dedicated access point installed in House 143. Users are relying on the access point from the adjacent room, which may result in weak or unstable wireless connectivity.

Outcome:
House 143 users will have improved and stable wireless connectivity through the installation of a dedicated in-wall access point, reducing dependency on the access point from the adjacent room.

Success Criteria:
  • A dedicated in-wall access point is successfully installed in House 143.
  • Users in House 143 can connect to Wi-Fi with stable signal strength.
  • Wireless connectivity no longer depends primarily on the access point from the adjacent room.
  • Internet access is tested and confirmed working after installation.
  • The installed access point is properly configured, adopted, and visible in the network management system.
fields.Requirements:
1. In-wall access point
    • One dedicated in-wall AP must be available for installation in House 143.
  2. Network connection
    • A working LAN/network line must be available at the installation point.
    • The port should provide network access and proper connectivity to the main network.
  3. Power source / PoE
    • The in-wall AP must receive power through PoE or the required compatible power source.
  4. Controller access
    • Access to the network management system/controller is required for AP adoption, configuration, and monitoring.
  5. Configuration details
    • Required SSID, VLAN, IP assignment, and access settings must be confirmed before deployment.
  6. Installation location
    • The wall outlet or installation area must be suitable for mounting the in-wall AP and should provide proper Wi-Fi coverage for House 143 users.
  7. Testing device
    • A laptop or mobile device should be available for Wi-Fi connectivity and speed testing after installation.
fields.Who:
Team Lead: Mervin Mani — Overall coordination, supervision, configuration, and validation
Team Members: OJTs — Installation support, cable preparation, testing assistance, and documentation support
fields.Schedule:
DateActivity / Milestone
May 25, 2026 (Monday)Initial Work Brief draft preparation
May 26, 2026 (Tuesday)Acceptance Criteria draft preparation with the Acceptance Owner, and work distribution meeting with the implementor, Team Lead, and OJTs
May 28, 2026 (Thursday)IT Team Work Brief discussion
June 1–5, 2026Implementation of House 143 in-wall AP installation
fields.Scope:
Scope
• Install one dedicated in-wall access point in House 143.
• Configure the AP with the correct SSID/network settings.
• Test Wi-Fi signal, internet access, and basic connectivity.
• Document the installation details and test results.

Constraints
• Room access availability
       • Installation depends on access to House 143 and availability of the room/user.
• Facilities support
       • Any wall mounting, outlet access, or physical installation support may require coordination with Facilities/Maintenance.

fields.Title: Back Up Vimeo Videos to Synology NAS turbo02
fields.Why:
Purpose

Back up every video in the Vimeo library before the paid Vimeo plan renews on June 29, 2026. The organization plans to downgrade Vimeo to a free plan and needs a local archive of past leaders’ videos.

Outcome

The work owner will download all Vimeo videos in the highest quality available and save them to:

[turbo02 root]\archivesVimeo\

The work owner will organize the files by event and year where possible and create an Excel inventory sheet in the same folder.

The acceptance owner will accept the work when the downloaded video count on turbo02 matches the Vimeo library count, except for any videos logged as failed after one retry.
fields.AcceptanceCriteria:
DEL-001: Vimeo Videos Backed Up in turbo02

CheckGood Looks Like
AC-001[turbo02 root]\archives\20260601Vimeo\ exists on turbo02.
AC-002The folder contains downloaded Vimeo video files.
AC-003Videos are downloaded in the highest quality available from Vimeo.
AC-004Videos are grouped by event and year where that information is available.
AC-005Videos with unclear event or year information are stored in a clearly named holding folder.
AC-006Sample video files open or play from the NAS location.
AC-007The downloaded video count matches the Vimeo library count, except for videos logged as failed after retry in the inventory sheet.

DEL-002: Excel Backup Inventory Sheet

CheckGood Looks Like
AC-008backup_inventory.xlsx exists inside [turbo02 root]\archives\20260601Vimeo\.
AC-009The sheet has one row for each Vimeo video.
AC-010Each row includes the video title.
AC-011Each row includes event and topic when identifiable.
AC-012Each row includes speaker when identifiable.
AC-013Each row includes the Vimeo upload date.
AC-014Each row includes backup status.
AC-015Each row includes the saved filename or folder path.
AC-016Failed downloads show that the video was retried once before being marked as failed.
AC-017Rows with missing, uncertain, or failed information include notes.
AC-018The acceptance owner can verify the backup using the NAS folder and inventory sheet.
fields.Requirements:
Access

The work owner must have access through LastPass to:

  • Vimeo, with permission to view and download videos.
  • Synology NAS turbo02.
  • The archive path under [turbo02 root]\archives\.

Backup

IDRequirement
REQ-001Include every video in the Vimeo library.
REQ-002Download each video in the highest quality available.
REQ-003Store the backup under [turbo02 root]\archives\20260601Vimeo\.
REQ-004Organize files by event and year where the information is available.
REQ-005Use clear folder and file names.
REQ-006Place videos with unclear event or year information in a clearly named holding folder.
REQ-007Retry each failed download once.
REQ-008Log each video that fails after retry.
REQ-009Log missing or unclear metadata in the inventory sheet.
REQ-010Match the final NAS video count to the Vimeo library count, except for logged failures.

Inventory Sheet

Save the inventory file as:

[turbo02 root]\archives\20260601Vimeo\backup_inventory.xlsx

The inventory sheet must include these columns:

ColumnRequired Content
Video TitleVimeo video title.
Event + TopicEvent and topic, if known or reasonably identifiable.
SpeakerSpeaker name, if known.
Date Uploaded to VimeoUpload date shown in Vimeo.
Backup StatusDownloaded, Failed After Retry, Missing Metadata, or another clear status.
Backup LocationSaved filename or folder path.
NotesFailure reason, missing metadata, or classification assumption.
fields.Who:
Roles

RoleOwnerResponsibility
Work ownerMervin ManiDownload videos, organize files, create the inventory sheet, and log exceptions.
Acceptance ownerRyann MicuaCheck the backup folder, inventory sheet, exception log, and video counts.

Work Plan

  1. Confirm Vimeo and turbo02 access through LastPass.
  2. Confirm available storage on turbo02.
  3. Record the total Vimeo library video count.
  4. Create [turbo02 root]\archives\20260601Vimeo\.
  5. Download all Vimeo videos in the highest quality available.
  6. Save the videos to the backup folder.
  7. Organize videos by event and year where possible.
  8. Create backup_inventory.xlsx inside the backup folder.
  9. Add one inventory row per Vimeo video.
  10. Retry failed downloads once.
  11. Log failed downloads, missing metadata, and classification assumptions.
  12. Count downloaded video files on turbo02.
  13. Compare the NAS count with the Vimeo library count.
  14. Submit the folder and inventory sheet to the acceptance owner for review.

Done Means

  • All Vimeo videos are downloaded to turbo02, except videos logged as failed after one retry.
  • The backup lives under [turbo02 root]\archives\20260601Vimeo\.
  • Videos are organized by event and year where possible.
  • backup_inventory.xlsx is complete and stored in the backup folder.
  • The NAS video count matches the Vimeo library count, except for logged failures.
  • The acceptance owner confirms that the acceptance criteria pass.
fields.Schedule:
Dates

DateMeaning
June 4, 2026Target completion date
June 29, 2026Hard deadline before the next Vimeo billing cycle
fields.Scope:
In Scope

  • Download all videos in the Vimeo library.
  • Download the highest quality available from Vimeo.
  • Save all files under [turbo02 root]\archives\20260601Vimeo\.
  • Organize videos by event and year where possible.
  • Create backup_inventory.xlsx inside the backup folder.
  • Record one inventory row per Vimeo video.
  • Retry failed downloads once.
  • Log videos that still fail after retry.
  • Log missing or unclear metadata.
  • Verify that the NAS video count matches the Vimeo library count, except for logged failures.

Out of Scope

  • Downgrading the Vimeo account.
  • Changing Vimeo billing settings.
  • Deleting videos from Vimeo.
  • Editing, trimming, compressing, or transcoding videos.
  • Correcting metadata in Vimeo.
  • Building a streaming portal or public video library.
  • Moving videos to another online platform.

Constraints and Risks

Constraint or RiskRequired Handling
Vimeo renews on June 29, 2026.Complete the backup before June 29. Target June 4.
Videos may be large.Confirm NAS storage before or during the backup.
Downloads may fail.Retry once, then log the failure.
Metadata may be incomplete.Use best judgment and log assumptions.
Event or year may be unclear.Use a holding folder and note the issue in the inventory sheet.
Time is limited.Prioritize complete video download before refining organization.

fields.Title: Initial Opportunity Assessment
fields.Why:
Background:
An idea was raised to create or implement a system for maintaining an organizational address book. This work brief defines the assessment needed to determine whether the idea is worth pursuing, what problem it may solve, and what options may be considered before creating a full project proposal.

Outcome:
The opportunity assessment will provide a clear recommendation on whether an address book system is needed, what business value it may provide, what requirements should be considered, and whether the initiative should proceed to planning, deferment, or closure.
fields.Requirements:
1. The assessment must clarify the business need for an address book system.
  2. The assessment must identify who will use and maintain the address book.
  3. The assessment must define the minimum information fields needed, such as name, department, role, contact number, email address, and other relevant details.
  4. The assessment must consider data privacy, access control, and information accuracy.
  5. The assessment must compare practical options before recommending a solution.
  6. The recommendation must state whether to proceed, defer, or not proceed.
fields.Scope:
Scope:

1. Review the current process for maintaining and using address book information.
2. Identify issues, gaps, or inefficiencies in the current approach.
3. Gather basic requirements from intended users or stakeholders.
4. Identify possible solution options, such as spreadsheet, SharePoint list, existing system, or dedicated address book application.
5. Assess possible benefits, risks, effort, and limitations.
6. Prepare an opportunity assessment report with recommendation.

Out of Scope:

1. Full system implementation.
2. Procurement or subscription purchase.
3. Detailed system design.
4. User account provisioning.
5. Data migration or cleanup beyond assessment needs.
6. Final approval of a project budget.
